summary: My father, Tokutaro Nakanishi, came to America when he was 14 years old. He was a hardworking man who loved writing letters in Japanese and subscribed to the Nichi Bei Times daily. He was determined to be his own boss rather than work under his gambler father. During the war, he worked as a cook at Topaz War Relocation Center and later farmed land near Palo Alto and San Jose. He was very proud of owning land and was a true Issei who took farming seriously.

My mother, Sasako Nakanishi, came from Wakayama Prefecture to America when she was eight years old. She learned English quickly after starting third grade here, despite speaking only Japanese at home. She settled in Menlo Park and started a nursery business growing chrysanthemums and carnations. She subscribed to the San Jose afternoon newspaper and was one of the few Issei women to do so. She had a hard time during the war, especially with four young children.

My parents were originally from Wakayama Prefecture in Japan. My mother came here at eight years old with her mother and sister, while her father had come earlier. I have visited Wakayama often, especially the gravesite of my mother’s grandmother. Mostly, I stayed with my father’s relatives when I visited Japan. Wakayama holds a lot of family history for us.

My father was an avid reader of the Nichi Bei Times, subscribing to it daily. He read the Japanese section thoroughly and also the English section, which helped him stay informed and improve his English. It was a newspaper that connected him to the Japanese American community and culture. I grew up enjoying newspapers too, partly because of his influence.

My mother subscribed to the San Jose afternoon newspaper, but the morning paper was the San Jose Mercury News. We got both papers at home, which made us avid newspaper readers as children. The San Jose Mercury News was part of our daily life and helped us stay connected to local news. It was important especially before and after the war.

The San Jose News was the afternoon paper my mother subscribed to, while the San Jose Mercury News was the morning paper. We received both regularly and became avid readers. This was during the time before the war when my parents were running their nursery business. The newspapers helped us stay informed and connected to the community.

My mother settled in Menlo Park, California, where she and her family started a nursery growing chrysanthemums and carnations. The area around Menlo Park, including Redwood City, East Palo Alto, and Palo Alto, had many Japanese flower growers. The nursery business was important to my mother’s side of the family before the war. Menlo Park was where our family’s agricultural roots were strong.

Redwood City was part of the region where many Japanese flower growers operated before the war. My mother’s family was involved in nursery work in this area along with Menlo Park, East Palo Alto, and Palo Alto. They grew chrysanthemums and carnations in cheesecloth houses to protect the flowers. Redwood City was part of the agricultural community that supported our family’s livelihood.

East Palo Alto was where my mother’s older brother bought property and built greenhouses for growing carnations. The greenhouses were carefully managed to protect the delicate flowers from too much sun and cold. This was part of the family’s flower growing business before the war. East Palo Alto was an important place for our family’s agricultural endeavors.

After the war, my family farmed land in Palo Alto near Stanford University and what became the Stanford Industrial Park. It was difficult to find land to rent or buy because of discrimination, but we managed to lease and later buy land. My father worked hard clearing the land and farming it properly. Palo Alto was a place where we rebuilt our lives and farmed seriously after returning from camp.

I was born in Santa Barbara along with my three siblings. My father leased hillside land there to farm peas and tomatoes, which could survive without well water. The land was considered marginal, but my father’s ingenuity and hard work made it productive. Santa Barbara was where our family started before the war interrupted our lives.

I was very young when the Pearl Harbor attack happened in December 1941, so I don’t remember much directly. I do recall that shortly after, a Japanese two-man submarine lobbed shells at an oil storage facility near our ranch in California. The shells were meant to scare people but didn’t explode much. This event caused fear and rumors of further attacks, leading my family to move inland soon after.

California was home for my family before, during, and after the war. We lived in places like Santa Barbara, Menlo Park, Palo Alto, and San Jose. The state had many Japanese immigrants involved in farming and nursery businesses. California was both a place of opportunity and hardship, especially during the war years with internment and discrimination.

My father had two older sisters who lived in Centerville, now part of Fremont, California. They owned property there, which was important because it gave us a place to go when we had to leave our home after Pearl Harbor. Their support helped our family during difficult times. Centerville was a safe haven for us during the war.

We were sent to the Tanforan Assembly Center in May 1942, shortly after Pearl Harbor. I was about five years old, and my siblings were very young. We stayed there only three or four months before being moved to Topaz. Tanforan was the first stop in the forced relocation and internment process for our family.

We arrived at Topaz in October 1942 and stayed through the war. My father worked as a cook there, and my grandfather cleaned grease traps. In the summer of 1943, my father briefly worked on a farm in Montana but had to return because of our young children. Topaz was a difficult time, but we made connections with others who later helped us after the war.

In the summer of 1943, my father worked on a farm in Montana while we were interned at Topaz. He couldn’t stay long because he had four young children at camp. The work was a way to save money and prepare for life after the war. Montana was a brief but important part of my father’s efforts to support our family.

After the war, some friends from Block 16 at Topaz settled in Walnut Creek, California. My father encouraged me to work for the Okumura family there, who were flower growers. Walnut Creek became a place where we reconnected with friends and found work in the flower growing business. It was part of rebuilding our lives after the camps.

The Okumura family were flower growers in Walnut Creek and friends from Topaz. After the war, my father recommended I work for them because they had a house and farm work. They grew orchids, which were challenging to cultivate in California’s climate. The Okumuras helped us transition back to civilian life and farming.

Hawaii was mentioned as a place where orchids were mostly grown because of its warm climate. The Okumura family in Walnut Creek tried to grow orchids, but California winters were too cold. Hawaii represented the ideal environment for some of the flowers my family and friends were interested in cultivating. It was a contrast to the challenges we faced farming in California.

My two older sisters bought a grocery store in Mountain View called Capsule City Market around 1946 or 1947. The store sold Japanese goods and served the local community. This was shortly after we came out of the camps and started rebuilding our lives. Mountain View was an important place for our family’s business ventures post-war.

Capsule City Market was the grocery store my sisters bought in Mountain View after the war. It specialized in Japanese goods and was well known in the community. The store was part of our family’s efforts to reestablish ourselves after internment. It represented a successful business venture in the late 1940s.

The land we farmed in Palo Alto was next to Stanford University and part of what became the Palo Alto Industrial Park. Finding land to rent or buy was difficult due to discrimination, but we managed to lease and eventually purchase property there. The area was transforming from farmland to industrial use, but we held on to our farming roots. It was a challenging but important place for our family’s post-war recovery.

After farming in Palo Alto, we also found land to farm in San Jose, which we were able to buy. San Jose was part of our family’s efforts to rebuild and own property after the war. It was a place where we could establish stability and continue our agricultural work. The community there was important for our family’s future.

entity: Japanese American Museum of San Jose
summary: The Japanese American Museum of San Jose is a place that preserves the history and stories of families like mine. Although not directly mentioned in my personal story, it represents the community and heritage that we are part of. It helps keep alive the memory of the internment camps and the resilience of Japanese Americans in California. It is an important cultural institution for our community.

Mune Farms was not directly mentioned in my story, but it is part of the broader Japanese American farming community in California. Families like mine worked hard to cultivate marginal land and grow flowers and crops despite challenges. Farms like Mune Farms represent the perseverance and dedication of Issei and Nisei farmers. They are part of the legacy we carry forward.
